context_lines: In **John 1**, the Gospel opens by identifying Jesus as the eternal Word through whom all things were made and who became flesh. The chapter establishes from the start that everything to follow must be read in light of Christ's divine identity, revelation, and saving mission.
explanation_lines: - **Jerome (c. 347-420 AD):** Jerome, translating the Gospels into Latin roughly a century and a half after Origen, made an unusual choice. In Luke's version of the Lord's Prayer he translated *epiousios* (eh-pee-OOH-see-os) with the ordinary Latin word for "daily." But in Matthew's version of the same Greek word, he instead coined a new Latin word that did not previously exist — *supersubstantialem*, "supersubstantial." Jerome explained his own reasoning directly: he understood this bread as bread "above all other substances, more excellent than all creatures" — and he identified it specifically with the body of the Lord. In other words, Jerome's choice of word was not a translation difficulty he stumbled into by accident. It was a deliberate theological judgment that the bread Jesus had in mind in Matthew 6:11 was not ordinary food at all, but Christ Himself. Like Origen before him, Jerome arrived at the same conclusion this document reaches through John 6 — working from the Greek text itself, more than a thousand years before anyone had reason to argue the point for any other purpose.

Both men were not guessing. Working directly from the Greek centuries before this study began, Origen and Jerome independently reached the same conclusion reached here through John 6 — that "daily bread" was never meant to be a request for food. This reading is not a modern reinterpretation forced onto the text. It predates the tradition that later flattened the verse into a simple request for material provision. Returning to the original language and the earliest readers does not introduce something new into the text — it recovers what was already there before tradition obscured it.
